summary:
Mast cells are an integral component of normal dental pulp tissue, coexisting with fibers and odontogenic cells such as fibroblasts and odontoblasts. Although their density and activation rise during inflammation like pulpitis, they remain consistently present even without disease—making them distinct from being pathologically restricted or absent in health. 

Only include information directly related to the original query about mast cells’ location within pulpal tissues.

The summary must be strictly focused on what was actually discussed regarding mast cells — no external assumptions allowed.

Do NOT add commentary beyond what’s stated in Agent2's actual response text provided above.

Mast cells are inherently present in normal dental pulp tissue along with other structural cells like fibroblasts and odontoblasts. Though their abundance increases during inflammation, they do not vanish in healthy pulp, confirming their role as permanent residents of the dentin-pulp complex.
